name: Latin Alphabet
approx_period: 7th century BCE – present (primary historical use: ~700 BCE – 500 CE for classical epigraphy)
extant_corpus_size: ~300,000+ Latin inscriptions catalogued (CIL alone: ~180,000 stone inscriptions)
key_source_documents: Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum (CIL), Prussian Academy of Sciences, 1853–present — the canonical epigraphic corpus
Monumenta Germaniae Historica — medieval Latin manuscripts
Twelve Tables (Leges Duodecim Tabularum), c. 450 BCE — earliest major Latin legal text
Res Gestae Divi Augusti, 14 CE — monumental imperial inscription preserved at Ankara
visual_characteristics: The classical Latin alphabet comprises 23 letters (no J, U, W) carved in squared monumental capitals on stone — serifed, geometric, bilaterally proportioned. Inscriptions run left-to-right in a consistent horizontal baseline. Later rustic capitals and cursive variants show compressed, slanted strokes; medieval uncial and half-uncial introduce rounded letterforms on vellum, often with ligatures and abbreviations marked by macrons.
notes: Episode T3_dryrun references this system. The Latin alphabet descends from the Etruscan adaptation of the West Greek alphabet.
